title: Hormuz Ships Move Again, but 80 Mines Remain — Oil Prices Slide as Demand Signals Diverge
subhead: The US-Iran deal is signed, and tankers are transiting Hormuz via cleared side channels — but the main shipping lane is still mined, and full recovery will take weeks. Oil fell below $78 on the deal news even as OPEC insists demand won't peak and PetroChina says Chinese consumption is dropping nearly 5%. Ukraine hit Moscow's main refinery again, and the G7 moved on critical minerals and Russian sanctions.

sections.heading: Hormuz: Ships Transiting, but the Main Channel Is Still Mined
sections.body:
For the first time in 110 days, major commercial vessels are transiting the Strait of Hormuz after the US-Iran agreement signed Wednesday at Versailles. Tankers operated by Grimaldi Group, Cosco, Knutsen, and NYK have passed through, according to Lloyd's List Intelligence. Two Iran-flagged National Iranian Tanker Company vessels have also entered the strait.

But the recovery is partial. Phillip Belcher, marine director of Intertanko, the global tanker owners' association, said the main central route remains closed with an estimated 80 mines still needing clearance. Ships are currently using two smaller channels — a northern route through Iranian waters and a southern route through Omani waters — which Belcher said 'now seem to be fully open.'

Vice President JD Vance said more than 12.5 million barrels moved through the strait on Wednesday night, calling it the highest flow since the war began. Two Iranian oil tankers carrying a combined 3.8 million barrels left Iranian ports and crossed the US blockade without interception, according to shipping trackers cited by Vance.

Germany is deploying two warships to the Red Sea in preparation for a possible Hormuz mission, Defence Minister Boris Pistorius announced Thursday. France and the UK have already committed to a multinational demining and naval escort operation.

Goldman Sachs projects Persian Gulf exports will return to pre-conflict levels by end of July, but warns that shipowner risk aversion could slow the recovery.
sections.whyItMatters: The strait is reopening, but via workarounds, not a clean return. An estimated 80 mines on the main route means the full-capacity transit corridor is still offline. Until demining is complete, throughput will remain below the pre-war ~140 ships per day.

sections.heading: Oil Prices Fall on Deal News, but Demand Outlooks Conflict Sharply
sections.body:
August Brent crude fell $2.55 to $77/bbl and July WTI dropped $2.69 to $74.10/bbl at midday Thursday, as the US-Iran deal signaled the return of trapped barrels to the market. US gasoline prices fell below $4/gal nationally for the first time since March, according to AAA data.

But the demand picture is increasingly bifurcated. OPEC's 2026 World Oil Outlook, released Thursday, maintained its forecast of global oil demand reaching 113.3 million bpd by 2030 — unchanged from last year — and said there is no sign demand will peak by 2050. The cartel cited policy shifts in the US and Europe toward energy affordability and security, slower EV adoption, and growth in India, the Middle East, and Africa. OPEC also said US tight crude production peaked at 9 million bpd in 2025 and sees only modest US liquids growth of 400,000 bpd until 2030 before plateauing.

The counterpoint came from PetroChina's research unit, which forecast that China's oil consumption will drop 4.9% in 2026 to 753 million tons, driven by the pivot to cleaner energy and war-driven high oil prices. PetroChina projects demand leveling off and then falling to about 700 million tons by 2030. Even so, China plans to add 15 million tons per year of new refining capacity.

IATA's June report highlighted the aviation impact: jet fuel crack spreads hit a record $80/bbl in April, and the global airline industry's net profit is projected to fall 49% year-over-year to $23 billion in 2026, with margins collapsing to just 2%.
sections.whyItMatters: Oil is falling on supply returning, but the demand story is splitting: OPEC says no peak, PetroChina says China is already declining, and IATA shows the war has devastated airline profitability. The 2027 glut the IEA flagged is meeting a Chinese demand picture that could be weaker than anyone projected.

sections.heading: Ukraine Hits Moscow Refinery Again as Russia Starts Importing Gasoline
sections.body:
Ukraine launched one of its largest drone attacks on Moscow since the war began on Thursday, with Russia's defense ministry reporting 555 drones shot down across the country — 180 around Moscow alone, according to Mayor Sergei Sobyanin. Several drones struck the Gazprom Neft Moscow oil refinery in the Kapotnya district for the second time this week, sending fireballs and thick black smoke over the capital. All four Moscow airports suspended operations.

The refinery had already been shut down after a Tuesday drone strike, according to Reuters sources. Tatneft also announced nationwide fuel purchase caps after strikes on its Taneco refinery in Tatarstan. At least 17 people were injured in Moscow, and a drone hit a shopping center, a high-rise apartment building, and an industrial facility.

Reuters reported that Russia is set to import gasoline by sea this month for the first time in modern memory, as Ukrainian drone attacks on refineries have created a domestic fuel shortage. Kpler analyst Nikhil Dubey said drones are now targeting hydrocracker units — the most complex and hardest-to-replace equipment — which take weeks or months to repair.

President Zelenskyy called the strikes 'fully justified' and said: 'If Ukraine burns, your Moscow will burn.' Kremlin aide Yuri Ushakov said the attacks push back prospects for any direct Putin-Zelenskyy meeting.
sections.whyItMatters: A net oil exporter importing gasoline by sea is a remarkable reversal. Ukraine's drone campaign has shifted from symbolic strikes to a systematic campaign targeting the most expensive and hardest-to-replace refinery equipment. Russia's export mix is shifting: more crude, less refined product — and the domestic fuel picture is deteriorating.

sections.heading: G7 Targets China's Rare Earth Dominance, But Floor Price Plan Stalls
sections.body:
G7 leaders agreed Wednesday to set up a critical minerals alliance and cooperation platform to reduce reliance on China, coordinating stockpiling and expanding the role of the International Energy Agency in supply chain monitoring. The group set a target of capping single-country rare earth imports below 60% by 2030, down from China's current ~70% share of mining and ~90% of processing.

But Trump's proposed floor-price mechanism for critical minerals failed to secure European backing at the summit, according to Reuters. The Pentagon-drafted plan, developed with AI assistance, would have established minimum prices to counter Chinese market-distortion practices. European governments called for medium- and long-term impact studies before committing, and the proposal stalled.

China, meanwhile, defended its export controls on critical minerals after the G7 statement, urging the group to 'respect international trade rules.' On Monday, new Chinese regulations took effect that bring mineral resources further under state control, granting the State Council more authority over exploration, mining, processing, stockpiling, and trade. The rules introduce a national catalog of mineral classifications mirroring Western critical minerals lists.

The IEA says China is the leading refiner for 19 of 20 strategic minerals it tracks, with an average market share of about 70%.
sections.whyItMatters: The G7 is aligning on the diagnosis — China's dominance in critical minerals is a national security risk — but diverging on the prescription. Without floor prices or offtake guarantees, new Western mines and refineries remain unbankable. China's simultaneous tightening of state control over minerals is moving faster than the G7's voluntary coordination.

sections.heading: Trump Signals Return of Russian Oil Sanctions; G7 Tightens Shadow Fleet Pressure
sections.body:
President Trump said the US could soon reimpose sanctions on Russian oil shipments that were eased during the Iran war to help lower prices. 'Soon we'll be able to do that because the oil is now flowing,' Trump told reporters at the G7. The Treasury Department issued a 30-day waiver in March allowing purchase of Russian crude already at sea; the waiver has since been extended.

The UK announced new sanctions targeting Russia's shadow fleet and finance networks, including vessels linked to the sanctioned Arctic LNG 2 project. Canada announced sanctions on 162 individuals, entities, and vessels linked to Russia's shadow fleet and energy sector. The G7 reached consensus on tougher sanctions targeting Russia's oil and gas exports, particularly the shadow fleet, according to CFR's Liana Fix.

Russia's fossil fuel export revenues actually rose 2% in May to €726 million per day, CREA reported, driven by higher prices. China remained the top buyer at €7 billion, India at €5.8 billion, and Turkey at €2.8 billion. Flows through the Druzhba pipeline to Hungary and Slovakia have resumed, boosting pipeline oil revenues by 22%.
sections.whyItMatters: The Iran crisis temporarily aligned US and Russian oil interests — Washington needed more supply, Moscow needed buyers. With Hormuz reopening, that alignment dissolves. Whether Trump actually lets the Russian waivers lapse, and whether Europe enforces new shadow fleet sanctions against its own buyers, will determine if this round has more bite than previous ones.

sections.heading: Aviation Still Bleeding from the Hormuz Shutdown
sections.body:
Even as the strait reopens, IATA's June report shows the damage to aviation is far from over. Jet fuel prices roughly doubled since late February, and crack spreads hit a record $80/bbl in April. The strait closure knocked roughly 10 million bpd of crude off the market — and 15 million bpd including refined products and LNG — with spot crude briefly touching $150/bbl.

Hormuz-linked shipments account for roughly one-fifth of global seaborne jet fuel trade, and the disruption left airlines in Europe, parts of Asia, and the US West Coast competing for scarce supply. IATA projects global airline net profit will fall 49% to $23 billion in 2026, with margins collapsing to 2%. Global passenger traffic growth is expected to slow to 2.1%, and cargo demand to just 0.7%.

IATA Director General Willie Walsh called the closure 'one of the most severe energy supply shocks in modern history.' Even with the cease-fire, the report warns that untangling supply chains, replenishing inventories, and restoring refinery operations will take weeks if not longer.
sections.whyItMatters: The war's impact on aviation will outlast the diplomatic resolution. Airlines are locked into higher fuel costs, disrupted routes, and reduced demand — and full recovery depends on how quickly mine-clearance restores normal Hormuz traffic, not on the signing of a deal.

bottomLine: The US-Iran deal is real and tankers are moving through Hormuz again — but via narrow side channels while an estimated 80 mines remain on the main route. Oil dropped below $78 on the deal news, yet the demand outlook is fracturing: OPEC says no peak to 2050, PetroChina says Chinese consumption is falling nearly 5% this year, and airlines face a 49% profit collapse. Ukraine's drone war on Russian refineries has made Russia a gasoline importer, the G7 is pushing on critical minerals and shadow fleet sanctions but split on Trump's floor-price plan, and Trump is signaling Russian oil waivers could lapse now that Hormuz is reopening. The deal changed the direction of the market — but the gap between signing and recovery is measured in weeks, and the aftershocks in months.
sources: Reuters, AP, NPR, Defense News, Lloyd's List Intelligence, Intertanko, IATA, OPIS/MarketWatch, OPEC World Oil Outlook 2026, PetroChina Research Institute, CREA, Newsweek, TechRepublic, India TV News, LiveMint, NBC News, Firstpost, CNBC, Yahoo Finance, World Energy News, The Telegraph
